I think we should optimize this -- this is exactly what re.compile('(your|list|of|items|here)').split() is good for -- it will push the search for characters that need to be encoded down into the regex C engine where they will be more efficient. You can precompile the compile and the split function, and can also precompile a dictionary that maps each character to its encoded value. Then the code to do this would look something like: 
    split_by_bad = split(obj)
    if len(split_by_bad) != 1:
        split_by_bad[1::2] = (lookup_replacements[x] for x in split_by_bad[1::2])
        obj.encoded = ''.join(split_by_bad)

```python
    loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

    request.addfinalizer(teardown)

    return loop
```

It's basically the second test case in the `SubTasks` section - that fails without this change. The basic problem is that the `old_pos` can be changed by the saving of another object without the object here being reloaded - "formset" mode. This happens when using both `OrderableTabularInline` and the `reorder` view on the list page. You won't see it when not using `unique_together` as there is eventual uniqueness of the ids.

More detail:
Suppose 4 objects (ABCD) with sort orders 1-4. Load objects AB from the database, and set them to have sort orders 3 and 4 respectively and save them. The following happens:
- A is moved out of the way (position 5)
- BC are moved down one place (B to 1, C to 2)
- A is moved back to position 3

So far so good. We now have BCAD. Now we save B with sort order 4, but it has `old_pos=2` because it has not been loaded from the database. So:
- B is moved out of the way (position 5)
- A is moved down one place (as it is in position 3). This clashes with the new position of C and throws an integrity error.
- (Now) we catch that, recheck the current position of B and move everything as normal.

The whole process is still imperfect at this level as we now have CADB which isn't what we asked for, kinda. However in the context where multiple values are assigned, it is most likely we are assigning all four items to new places as a reordering view. i.e. C to position 1 and D to position 2.

Saving C (from position 3 to position 1) we would try to move positions 1 and 2 up, but of course D is now in C's position so this fails as well and we recheck that C is in fact in the correct place and we have nothing to do.

Saving D (from position 4 to position 2) would not hit an error in this case as only A would be moved (back to its correct position 3) and D would go 3-5-2. We then have CDAB as requested.

I don't know if there is a permutation which can result in the wrong order after all of these have been applied - it is quite possible but I can't be bothered to work out the maths to try and prove it. It would be better if there was an API for explicitly setting the ordering which was used by the relevant places in the admin. This is one of the things I want to add.

I'm a little concerned about this.  It would be nice if there was a calculation to show that the linear approximation you use for k < kmin has errors less than kvalue_accuracy.  We usually try to make sure of that, and it's not obvious to me that this value for kmin satisfies that criterion.  

However, I've also proposed getting rid of the truncation option, which means this whole function would go away.  In which case the comment is moot.

I don't think this calculation is right.  First, a straightforward reading of the derivation would imply that you should have cosi**2 here.

But more importantly, this calculation too conservative.  Particularly in the case where cosi ~= 0.  In particular, it's not really appropriate to ignore the convolution factor, since it is significant precisely for ky >> 1, which is the limit you are taking for this calculation.  

I think you can probably do a few iterations of a non-linear solver to find where

(1+ky^2 cosi^2)**-1.5 * (_half_pi_h_sini_over_r * ky) / sinh(_half_pi_h_sini_over_r * ky) ~= epsilon

As I mentioned earlier, you'll want to do this separately for epsilon = kvalue_accuracy and maxk_threshold for two quantities _ksq_max and _maxk.  I think you'll find that you don't need to go out to nearly as high a ky value as this calculation would imply.  Particularly when cosi << 1.

UTF-8 is actually marginally faster at parsing than utf-8

JSON-decoding is two-step process. First, it needs to convert from bytes to characters (symbols). Thi is done by UTF-8 (typically) decoder in `.text()`. Second-step -- is to decode slash-escapes according to JSON-format. this is done using `loads()`. So, encoding you are talking about is for first step only. As you can see, letter `u` after every slash signifies that every item is UNICODE symbol (as opposed to some byte value). And so encoding is not applicable.

```
class A(orb.Table):

    value = orb.StringColumn(shortcut='b.c.d')

class B(orb.Table):
    c = orb.ReferenceColumn('C')

class C(orb.Table):
     d = orb.StringColumn()
```

doing:

```
a = A(1)
```
will now auto-expand through b and c to get the d column value.
